critic_raw:
This multi-modal immunotherapy response prediction experiment demonstrates strong methodological attention in preprocessing discipline, label quality (RECIST 1.1 with radiologist confirmation), and the use of AUROC as a threshold-agnostic primary metric. However, the design has several significant limitations that constrain the validity and interpretability of results.

The most critical concern is severe underpowering. A 20% held-out test set from 500 patients yields approximately 100 test subjects, with an anticipated responder rate (CR+PR) of perhaps 30-50% in melanoma immunotherapy, leaving 30-50 positive cases in the test set. Confidence intervals on AUROC with this sample size are extremely wide (easily spanning 0.15-0.20 on each side), making it impossible to reliably distinguish clinically meaningful performance differences between the multi-modal model and the genomic-only baseline. No sample size or power calculation is reported to justify this allocation.

The choice of stratified random rather than temporal splitting deserves scrutiny. In a clinical dataset spanning 5 years, treatment protocols, patient selection criteria, and response rates have likely evolved over time as the field of melanoma immunotherapy advanced rapidly during this period. A stratified random split would allow the model to train on late-data patterns and be tested on earlier patients, or vice versa, creating temporal leakage and an unrealistically optimistic evaluation compared to prospective deployment.

The BioBERT encoder is described as 'not retrained on this cohort to avoid label leakage,' but this reasoning conflates two distinct concepts. Using a pre-trained encoder is appropriate, but the concern about label leakage would apply to fine-tuning on the outcome-labeled cohort, not to using a frozen encoder trained on generic biomedical text. The rationale as stated is technically imprecise and may signal an incomplete understanding of the leakage mechanism.

The baseline model uses genomic features only, while the proposed multi-modal model adds clinical tabular features and clinical text. This means the comparison tests the value of additional data modalities combined with a multimodal fusion architecture, not the value of multi-modal learning per se. An ablation adding each modality incrementally would be required to attribute performance differences.
all_issues_raised: Test set of ~100 patients is severely underpowered; AUROC confidence intervals with 30-50 positive cases are too wide to detect clinically meaningful differences
Stratified random split over a 5-year period allows temporal leakage in a domain where treatment protocols evolved significantly; temporal split would be more appropriate
Rationale for not fine-tuning BioBERT conflates label leakage with general pre-training; argument as stated is technically imprecise
Baseline uses genomic features only while proposed model adds two additional modalities plus fusion architecture, conflating feature access with architectural contribution
No sample size or power calculation provided to justify the 500-patient cohort and 60/20/20 allocation for achieving reliable AUROC estimates
Insufficient sample size (n=500; ~30-40 test positives) for stable AUROC estimation — no power analysis or minimum detectable effect size presented
Genomic-only neural network baseline is not clinically meaningful; standard clinical practice baseline (logistic regression on TMB, MSI, ECOG, LDH) is absent
Clinical notes may contain interim response indicators documented before formal RECIST assessment, creating potential label leakage through text features
Batch correction for multi-site genomic data may be underpowered given small total sample size; site as residual confound is not fully controlled
Validation set of n~100 is too small for stable hyperparameter selection via grid search or Bayesian optimization, risking high-variance model selection
Insufficient test set size (n=100) to reliably distinguish AUROC values in the clinically relevant range; statistical power analysis is absent
Stratified random split does not test robustness to temporal distribution shift across the 5-year collection period as treatment protocols and genomic panels evolved

Neural network used as genomics-only baseline may underperform its potential due to overfitting at small n; a simpler baseline (logistic regression, gradient boosting) would better represent current clinical practice
Frozen BioBERT embeddings pretrained on biomedical literature may not transfer to immunotherapy clinical notes; no ablation tests whether text branch helps or harms fusion
No correction for multiple secondary metrics (AUPRC, ECE, kappa) in the reporting strategy; increased risk of spurious findings on the small test set
